---
title: "Research"
pagetitle: "Research — Arvind Karunakaran"
description: "Research on authority and accountability in the workplace, professions, organization theory, and technological and organizational change."
---

::: {.lede}
My research examines **authority and accountability in the workplace**,
especially in the context of technological change. I am currently focused on
**human–AI augmentation** in the workplace — how emerging AI systems reshape
the authority of professionals, the accountability of organizations, and the
distribution of skill and opportunity at work.
:::

## Approach

I use **ethnographic and field-based methods** — participant observation and
interviews — as my primary lens, complemented by **comparative-historical
analysis** of archival data and **computational analysis** of large text
corpora. Combining these approaches lets me trace how work is actually done on
the ground while situating those practices within longer historical arcs and
larger populations of cases.

## Areas

::: {.stack-list}
- **Sociology of work, occupations, and professions.** How occupational groups
  claim, contest, and lose authority; how the boundaries between professions
  are drawn and redrawn.
- **Organization theory.** Accountability, coordination, and control inside and
  across organizations — including meso-level dynamics between individuals,
  groups, and institutions.
- **Technological and organizational change.** How new technologies —
  platforms, social media, and AI — reconfigure work, authority, and
  accountability, and how organizations respond.
:::

## Topics

::: {.stack-list}
- **Authority in the workplace.** The bases of workplace authority and how
  status asymmetries between professions shape collaboration and conflict.
- **Professional, organizational, and algorithmic accountability.** How
  accountability is produced, obscured, and redistributed as evaluation moves
  from institutions to crowds and algorithms.
- **Human–AI augmentation and reskilling.** How AI systems augment (or
  displace) professional judgment, and the reskilling that augmentation demands.
- **Social media scrutiny.** How public, crowd-based scrutiny reconfigures the
  accountability of frontline professionals and the organizations that employ
  them.
:::

## Selected Themes in Recent Work

Several recent papers develop the idea of **obscured accountability** — the
ways in which social-media scrutiny and platform intermediation can diffuse
responsibility rather than concentrate it (*ASQ* 2024; *Organization Science*
2022). A parallel line of work examines **status–authority asymmetry** between
adjacent professions (*ASQ* 2022), and a growing body of collaborative work
maps the **impact of AI on workplace inequality** and the governance of
**platform power** (*Academy of Management Annals* 2024, 2025).

[See all publications →](publications.qmd)
